_meta.purpose: Vocal tonal research corpus. Goal: understand where each master's voice sits relative to active mbira scale degrees at each chord position. This becomes the vocal constraint model — same architecture as the note constraint engine, but for voice.
_meta.research_method: Pitch-detect vocal line against active mbira notes per beat. Build dataset: [artist, tuning, progression, chord_position, beat] → vocal pitch interval from root.
_meta.status: catalogue complete — pitch analysis pending (requires audio isolation per video)

living_masters.name: Chris Berry
living_masters.vocal_style: Shona ceremonial singing absorbed over years of all-night bira ceremonies in Zimbabwe. Deep tonal alignment with mbira scale — not ornamental singing over the top, but voice as another tine. Platinum album sales in Zimbabwe and Mozambique.
living_masters.tonal_character: Mid-to-high register, gravitates toward scale degrees 4 and 5 on Nyamaropa. Strong melodic resolution on root. Call-and-response emphasis.

sources.title: Mbira Playing Chris Berry
sources.note: Solo mbira + voice, clearest voice-mbira isolation. PRIMARY analysis candidate.

sources.title: Chris Berry and Panjea Promo Video
From Traditional Shona Music to Electric Mbira: A Demo
sources.note: Demonstrates traditional-to-electric continuum. Good for tonal analysis.

living_masters.analysis_priority: HIGH — clearest voice-mbira relationship in contemporary playing; extensive documented repertoire

living_masters.name: Nyamasvisva
living_masters.vocal_style: Founder, lead singer, composer, arranger of Mawungira eNharira. Living custodian of mbira dzavadzimu tradition. Plays many different progressions for same songs — highly variable harmonic approach.
living_masters.tonal_character: Traditional Shona ceremonial vocal placement. Closer to mbira scale root-fifth vocabulary than Chris Berry's more melodic approach.

sources.title: Taireva Mbira Tutorial with Nyamasvisva
sources.note: mbira.online lesson — voice and mbira clearly paired. ANALYSIS CANDIDATE.

living_masters.analysis_priority: HIGH — traditional root, plays across all progressions, already 5 songs in our song index

living_masters.name: Muda Magaya
living_masters.vocal_style: Deep traditional ceremonial singing. Known for technical and cultural commentary alongside playing. Pairs voice with kutsinhira interlocking patterns.
living_masters.tonal_character: Gravitates toward strong chord roots. Voice functions as third part in the kushaura-kutsinhira-voice triangle.

sources.title: Muda Magaya lessons on mbira.online
sources.note: 5 song lesson pages with vocal context. Source for analysis.

living_masters.analysis_priority: HIGH — 5 songs in index, vocal context documented in lesson pages

living_masters.name: Forward Kwenda
living_masters.vocal_style: Haunting, ceremonial. Associated with Tadzungaira vocal tradition — 'prayer to end all human suffering'. Demonstrates tuning shifts (Nyamaropa/Nemakonde) while maintaining vocal relationship to progression.
living_masters.tonal_character: Voice tracks the 'root feel' note of the active tuning/progression. On Nyamaropa Prog 4 — voice gravitates to degree 4. On Nemakonde Prog 6 — voice gravitates to degree 6. Key insight for tonal model.

sources.title: Forward Kwenda: Tadzungaira lesson page
sources.note: Vocal context described. Filmed at Great Zimbabwe.

living_masters.analysis_priority: MEDIUM — vocal context documented but fewer video sources currently catalogued

living_masters.name: Tute Chigamba
living_masters.vocal_style: Traditional ceremonial. 7 songs in our index. Known for playing Nyamaropa and Gandanga tunings in same session — demonstrates vocal adaptation across tuning shifts.
living_masters.tonal_character: Voice adapts to tuning shift — good comparative data source for Nyamaropa vs Gandanga vocal placement.

living_masters.name: Fradreck Mujuru
living_masters.vocal_style: Son of Ephat Mujuru. Has developed mbira vocals in recent years — adds 'heartfelt singing to complex intertwined melodies'. Bridge between Ephat's instrumental tradition and vocal tradition.
living_masters.tonal_character: Emerging — being developed as distinct voice. Good bridge data between deceased master (Ephat) and living practice.

deceased_masters.name: Stella Chiweshe
deceased_masters.title: Queen of Mbira
deceased_masters.vocal_style: Broke gender barrier — mbira was male-only instrument before her. Internationally celebrated voice + mbira dzavadzimu. Taught at Global Music Academy Berlin. Distinctive high soprano over traditional patterns.
deceased_masters.tonal_character: Higher register than male masters. Voice creates strong contrast with low mbira tines — best illustration of treble vocal over bass mbira. Ideal for register dial research.

sources.title: Stella Chiweshe - Live at Ufa Fabik (digitally restored)
sources.note: HIGHEST PRIORITY — restored archival, clearest voice+mbira separation. PRIMARY analysis candidate.

sources.title: Stella Chiweshe - live @ Club Gretchen 2020
sources.note: Voice + mbira + marimba + hosho + electric bass

sources.title: Stella Chiweshe - Chipindura
sources.note: Also in our song index (Tute Chigamba version). Cross-artist comparison possible.

deceased_masters.analysis_priority: HIGHEST — most documented deceased master, YouTube archive significant, tonal character strongly distinct

deceased_masters.name: Ephat Mujuru
deceased_masters.vocal_style: Primarily instrumental master — voice provided by Uncle Mude on recordings ('one of the greatest mbira singers of the 20th century'). The Mujuru ensemble vocal tradition is the key data here.
deceased_masters.tonal_character: Uncle Mude's vocals on Mbavaira represent the deep traditional vocalist-as-instrument approach. Voice blends into mbira texture rather than floating above it.

sources.title: Ephat Mujuru & Spirit of the People - Mbavaira (Full Album, 1983)
sources.note: Uncle Mude vocals. HIGHEST PRIORITY for deceased-master vocal analysis.

sources.title: Ephat Mujuru - Mbavaira (Awesome Tapes reissue, 84K views)
Ephat Mujuru - Journey of the Spirit (2002)
sources.note: Includes Nyamaropa, Dande — songs already in our index

sources.title: Mbira Music and Stories by Ephat Mujuru
The Technique of the Mbira Dza Vadzimu (Villon Films)
sources.note: 21-min technique film — HIGHEST PRIORITY for instrumental analysis

sources.title: An Old Cult Centre, Mbira dza Vadzimu, Muchatera Mujuru, Ephat Mujuru (Villon Films)

deceased_masters.analysis_priority: HIGHEST — 1983 recordings are pre-electric, Uncle Mude vocal + mbira dzavadzimu is clearest traditional vocal-instrument relationship in archive

deceased_masters.name: Uncle Mude
deceased_masters.vocal_style: Ephat Mujuru's vocalist. Described as 'one of the greatest mbira singers of the 20th century'. Voice blends into mbira texture — the ideal model of voice-as-tine.
deceased_masters.tonal_character: Deep traditional. Voice functions as an additional mbira part rather than a melody over accompaniment. This is the target model — voice constrained by the same harmonic system as the tines.

sources.title: Ephat Mujuru - Mbavaira (Full Album)
sources.note: Primary source for Uncle Mude vocal analysis

deceased_masters.analysis_priority: HIGHEST — closest documented example of voice constrained by mbira harmony rather than floating over it

deceased_masters.name: Sekuru Gora
deceased_masters.vocal_style: Pre-independence field recording era. One of the oldest documented mbira vocal traditions.
deceased_masters.analysis_priority: MEDIUM — oldest tradition, hardest to access

deceased_masters.name: Muchatera Mujuru
deceased_masters.vocal_style: Ephat Mujuru's father. Pre-independence ceremonial master. Featured in Villon Films documentary alongside Ephat.

sources.title: An Old Cult Centre - Muchatera & Ephat Mujuru (Villon Films, 52 min)

deceased_masters.analysis_priority: HIGH — multi-generational comparison with Ephat and Fradreck possible

tonal_hypothesis.core_principle: Mbira vocal placement is constrained by the same harmonic system as tine selection. Voice is not melody over accompaniment — it is an additional tine, subject to the same valid-note rules per chord position.
tonal_hypothesis.forward_kwenda_key_insight: Voice tracks the 'root feel' note of the active tuning/progression. Nyamaropa Prog 4 → voice gravitates to degree 4. Nemakonde Prog 6 → voice gravitates to degree 6. The root feel shifts with tuning, and so does the vocal center.
tonal_hypothesis.uncle_mude_key_insight: Voice-as-tine paradigm. Voice blends into texture rather than floating above. Target model for the AI vocal engine.
tonal_hypothesis.stella_chiweshe_key_insight: Higher register voice over lower tines — demonstrates how the vocal sits in treble register while mbira occupies bass/mid. Maps to register dial (0=bass tines dominant, 1=treble+voice dominant).
tonal_hypothesis.chris_berry_key_insight: Voice absorbed through all-night ceremony immersion, not theory study. Calls the valid note choices without naming them. This is the empirical confirmation that the constraint system is real — even a non-Shona player internalizes it through deep listening.

analysis_pipeline.step_1: Audio source selection — prioritize videos with clearest voice+mbira separation (flagged above as 'ANALYSIS CANDIDATE' or 'PRIMARY')
analysis_pipeline.step_2: Pitch detection — use librosa (Python) or CREPE model to extract vocal F0 contour per time segment
analysis_pipeline.step_3: Mbira note mapping — identify active chord position in 48-beat cycle at each time segment (use song index + progression map)
analysis_pipeline.step_4: Interval calculation — compute vocal pitch as interval above identified mbira root note at each position
analysis_pipeline.step_5: Dataset construction — [artist, tuning, progression, chord_position, beat_in_chord] → [vocal_interval_from_root]
analysis_pipeline.step_6: Pattern extraction — identify preferred intervals per chord position per artist → vocal constraint table
analysis_pipeline.step_7: Model integration — vocal constraint table feeds into audioEngine.js as a 7th voice channel, constrained same as tine channels

hugh_tracey_archive.note: Hugh Tracey field recordings 1950s-70s are the deepest pre-electric archive. Some on Smithsonian Folkways (free streaming). Search: 'Hugh Tracey mbira Zimbabwe'. These recordings have rawer voice+instrument separation than studio recordings — best ground truth.
